video_title: There's Going To Be One Hell Of A Hangover When The Market Party Ends | Louis Gave
gist: Louis Gave argues the 2025-26 global equity rally is almost entirely a semiconductor/AI-hardware trade whose capex math no longer adds up, urging investors to rotate into financials, value, metals and Latin America while hedging the two tail risks he sees: an AI bust and Japanese capital repatriation. He frames the world as an 'inflationary boom' where bonds stay dead and oil is range-bound 65-100 because China sets the price.

themes.theme: Semiconductors as the only trade that worked

insights.claim: 2025-26 global equity outperformance is almost entirely a semiconductor trade: own semis and you had a monster year, don't own them and you didn't, the same single-factor pattern across US and international markets.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Index-level returns mask a narrow trade; investors holding broad indices are effectively making a concentrated semiconductor bet they may not realize.
insights.quote: if you bought semiconductors, if you own semiconductor stocks, you've had a monster year and if and if you didn't, then you didn't

insights.claim: Semiconductors have risen from roughly 10% to 18% of the S&P 500 in about two years, with similar gains in the world MSCI, concentrating the index in the most cyclical and capital-intensive sector.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Passive index holders carry historic concentration in a hyper-cyclical sector right at peak enthusiasm, raising drawdown risk if the cycle turns.
insights.quote: in the US semiconductors are now 18% of the index which is kind of it's kind of mind-blowing

insights.claim: Three semiconductor names (TSMC, Samsung, SK Hynix) make up roughly a third of the Asian MSCI index, so Asia's outperformance disappears once those three stocks are stripped out.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: International diversification into Asia ex-semis offers far less of the 2025-26 return than headline indices suggest.
insights.quote: you now have three stocks in the Asian markets TSMC Samsung SKHEX that almost make up a third of the 30% of the index

insights.claim: The performance split was sector-driven globally: tech-hardware/semi holders 'laughed all the way to the bank' while software, healthcare, staples and consumer discretionary had a miserable year in the US.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Within tech, the dispersion between hardware and software was extreme; sector selection mattered far more than the growth-vs-value label.
insights.quote: If you're on tech hardware stocks, you've laughed all the way to the bank

insights.claim: The China-listed vs Hong Kong divergence (Shenzhen crushed it, Hong Kong down over 12 months) is largely explained by mainland indices carrying tech-hardware and semiconductor names that Hong Kong lacks.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Choice of China vehicle (onshore A-shares vs HK) drives returns through hardware exposure, not broad 'China' sentiment.
insights.quote: in China you have a fair amount of tech hardware stocks listed semiconductor names but other other guys in the tech hardware chain and in Hong Kong you don't

themes.theme: AI capex math doesn't add up

insights.claim: AI and AI-related stocks now comprise about 45% of the S&P 500 (Taggart's stat), making the index extraordinarily dependent on a single, cyclical technology theme.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Roughly half of S&P market cap is tied to AI; a theme correction would be an index-level event, not a sector rotation.
insights.quote: AI and AI related stocks now comprise 45% of the S&P, not the NASDAQ, but the S&P

insights.claim: McKinsey-cited projections call for $6.7 trillion of AI spending between now and 2030, which Gave estimates requires the AI industry to generate roughly $2 trillion of revenue every year to justify the capex given depreciation and margins.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A clear, falsifiable hurdle: track whether AI industry revenue is on a path toward ~$2T/yr; current run-rate is nowhere close.
insights.quote: between uh this year in 2030, we're supposed to spend $6.7 trillion in uh in AI

insights.claim: AI needs to generate about twice the entire global advertising industry's annual revenue (roughly $1 trillion) to justify projected capex, a gap Gave says it is nowhere near closing.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Anchoring AI's required revenue against the ~$1T global ad market makes the monetization gap concrete and skeptical of current valuations.
insights.quote: the global advertising industry is roughly one trillion a year. Uh, so you need the AI like right now to start making sense to start making twice the annual revenue of the the global advertising industry

insights.claim: AI capex accounted for more than three-quarters of the increase in US GDP growth, meaning the broader economy's strength is structurally tied to the durability of AI spending.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A slowdown in AI capex would directly subtract from headline GDP, linking an equity-theme correction to recession risk.
insights.quote: You look at AI capex, it basically accounted for more than 3/4 of the increase in GDP growth

insights.claim: AI infrastructure ages faster than prior buildouts: chips become outdated quickly and replacing the chips/equipment costs about two-thirds of building the data center in the first place, unlike railroads or dark fiber that sat dormant then got reused.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: High maintenance capex and short asset life undermine the 'overbuild now, monetize later' analogy used to justify spending.
insights.quote: the replacement cost of a data center, you know, replacing the chips and everything in there. It's about 2/3 the cost of building the data center in the first place

insights.claim: Late-1990s broadband buildout spent about $500 billion (roughly $900 billion in today's dollars), only about one-seventh of projected AI spending, and still produced bankruptcies at Nortel, Lucent and Global Crossing.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Historical precedent shows even a real technology revolution wipes out many infrastructure 'picks and shovels' names along the way.
insights.quote: Back then it was $500 billion. In today's dollar it's about $900 billion

insights.claim: Investors are buying semiconductors as the 'picks and shovels' AI proxy because there are few other ways to get exposure to AI products they personally find impressive, a FOMO-driven crowding dynamic.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: The semiconductor bid is partly a behavioral artifact of limited exposure options, vulnerable to reversal once new IPOs broaden the menu.
insights.quote: How do I get exposure to it in my uh in my portfolio And there have been very few ways to do that

themes.theme: Four-prisms discipline and the case to rotate out

insights.claim: Gave evaluates any investment through four prisms: fundamentals, momentum, investor positioning and valuation; on AI today fundamentals are exciting but stretched, momentum is strong, positioning is crowded, and valuations 'make no sense'.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A reusable scoring framework: AI scores poorly on two of four prisms, signaling a momentum trade rather than an edge-driven investment.
insights.quote: you have to look at it through four prisms. You have to look at the fundamentals... momentum... investor positioning and then finally you look at valuations

insights.claim: Gave argues continuing to hold AI is playing a momentum game in which he personally has no edge, and discipline means redeploying into less-crowded, cheaper opportunities even while others get rich.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI hold as a no-edge momentum bet; the actionable move is rotation toward asymmetric value elsewhere.
insights.quote: what you're essentially playing is a momentum game. And then... what hedge do I have in this momentum game? And and personally, I'm not personally I'm not sure I have any.

insights.claim: Gave concedes he has been wrong on AI: his own firm's Asian Opportunities manager Alfred Ho played the semiconductors and had a monster year while Gave was wary, an internal debate Gave admits losing.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A candid self-assessment that early bearishness was costly, tempering the conviction of his current caution.
insights.quote: he's been right and I've been wrong

insights.claim: Latin America offers a rerating opportunity in deeply undervalued local assets, driven by political rightward shifts (El Salvador, Argentina, Bolivia, Ecuador, Chile, Peru, and now Colombia), corporate governance improvements and growing local pension funds.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A concrete uncrowded rotation: market-supportive political change plus local pension flows argue for a structural rerating of LatAm assets.
insights.quote: there's a lot you know very exciting changes occurring all across Latin America political change corporate governance changes uh growth of local pension funds all of which argue for a rerating of local assets that are deeply deeply undervalued

insights.claim: Financials are doing well in most international markets (Japan, Korea, China, Canada) while US banks trade sideways, and Gave watches relative bank performance as a leading indicator of system health.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: International financials are a less-crowded place to redeploy capital, and bank relative performance is the tell for an approaching deflationary bust.
insights.quote: when you start to see banks meaningly underperform that's that's a that's a warning sign

insights.claim: Canadian financials are having a monster year despite expectations they would suffer from Canadian real estate troubles and a near-recession, a counterintuitive signal of underlying resilience.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Bank strength contradicts the bearish Canada macro narrative; worth watching as a contrarian indicator.
insights.quote: Canadian financials are having a monster year which is somewhat perhaps a little bit counterintuitive

themes.theme: Four-quadrant macro: inflationary boom is the base case

insights.claim: Gave's framework maps asset prices to four scenarios driven by changes in growth and inflation: deflationary boom (buy growth stocks), inflationary boom (buy value and metals), inflationary bust (buy energy), deflationary bust (buy government bonds).
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A portfolio-construction map: identify and eliminate the least-likely quadrant, then diversify across the rest.
insights.quote: If you're in a deflationary bust, you buy government bonds. If you're in a deflationary boom, you buy growth stocks. If you're an inflationary boom, you buy value stocks and metals. And if you're an inflationary bust, you buy energy.

insights.claim: Gave's base case is an inflationary boom, favoring value stocks, metals and financials (the latter via a steeper yield curve), because US deficits run ~7% of GDP in a boom and oil is unlikely to spike to $100-120.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Positions the portfolio for value/metals/financials rather than growth or bonds; the thesis breaks only on an oil spike or AI bust.
insights.quote: the highest odds are, you know, you're going to be in an inflationary boom

insights.claim: The deflationary bust is the least-likely scenario today because massive US fiscal stimulus (~7% of GDP deficits in a boom) plus a tsunami of private AI capex make a recession mathematically hard to reach.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Justifies eliminating the bond quadrant and staying invested in risk assets despite stretched AI valuations.
insights.quote: the least likely remains the deflationary bust because of today's fiscal policies, because of today's monetary policies

insights.claim: A deflationary bust requires banks to have over-lent into the boom; bank share performance is not currently flashing red, but Gave warns banks always appear unexposed until a bust reveals they funded the excess indirectly because money is fungible.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Monitor bank lending and relative share performance as the early-warning system for whether the AI boom is debt-funded.
insights.quote: somehow the banks are exposed because money is funible. They ended up lending to something else, but that money ended up going into AI

insights.claim: The inflationary-bust scenario was avoided because oil did not move to $120; with the situation resolved, Gave assigns low odds to an oil spike and therefore to an inflationary bust.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Reduces the case for an energy-overweight inflationary-bust positioning unless oil breaks above $100.
insights.quote: we had a chance at an inflationary bust if oil prices moved to 120 bucks but we didn't go there

themes.theme: Bonds are dead and the new portfolio

insights.claim: Gave maintains bonds have been dead as an asset class for five years and the 60/40 model is finished, advocating instead 60% equity, 20% energy, 20% metals as the 'new portfolio' from his 2021 book Avoiding the Punch.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A concrete allocation alternative to 60/40; the key active decisions become which metals, which equities and how to express energy.
insights.quote: what you now need is 60 equity, 20% energy, 20% uh 20% metals

insights.claim: The Fed has failed to hit its 2% inflation target for 64 months in a row, which Gave reads not as incompetence but as deliberate financial repression to inflate away ~100% debt-to-GDP.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: If above-target inflation is policy, real bond returns stay negative and the case for metals/value over bonds persists.
insights.quote: the Fed has now failed to meet its 2% inflation target for 64 months in a row

insights.claim: Western bond markets are following Japan's path: Gave expects roughly 300 basis points of yield-curve steepening (1- to 30-year) as the only way to manage debt, with bonds becoming interesting only if 10-year yields hit 6-7%.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Sets explicit re-entry triggers for bonds (6-7% 10-year) and frames steepeners/financials as the structural beneficiaries.
insights.quote: I think this is exactly what's going to happen in most of the western world

insights.claim: Capital is trapped in bond markets by ever-increasing regulation and creeping capital controls (e.g., French life-insurance policies steered into French/German bonds with no option to hold gold), preventing the bond exodus that valuations would otherwise warrant.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Explains why bond yields stay artificially low despite negative real returns; the structural bid is regulatory, not voluntary.
insights.quote: a lot of the money that's in bond market is stuck there for regulatory reasons that incidentally keep on increasing every year

insights.claim: Private markets ballooned from $1 trillion to $15 trillion in about 20 years on a promise of high double-digit returns with low volatility that, 15 years on, has been met by some managers but not the majority, partly because scale erodes returns.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Skeptical signal on private-market allocations held by pension funds; large pools likely face disappointing realized returns.
insights.quote: why the private markets grew from 1 trillion to 15 trillion in the space of essentially 20 years

themes.theme: Japan repatriation: the Godzilla risk and yen hedge

insights.claim: Japan holds roughly $3.5 trillion of US assets (about 10% of US GDP) and a large positive net foreign investment position (~+80%), versus the US at roughly -90%, so Japanese repatriation as yields rise is a major tail risk.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of a potential Japanese exit from US/European bonds; selling of treasuries and French OATs would pressure global bond markets.
insights.quote: three and a half trillion... just to put to put a number on it, that's that's 10% of US GDP

insights.claim: Gave sees Japanese capital repatriation as a story for the next 12 months, triggered by a cheap yen, rising JGB yields, a more hawkish inflation-fighting BOJ raising rates, and inflation becoming a political problem.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Places a near-term clock on the repatriation risk; watch BOJ rate moves and JGB yields as triggers.
insights.quote: I think it's a story for the next 12 months

insights.claim: Unlike Mike Green's slow-and-steady passive-flow 'robot', Japanese outflows would be fast and furious because a policy change (Korea-style tax incentive) or a GPIF repatriation decision would give every smaller Japanese pension fund green light to follow.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Repatriation could be a sudden, herd-driven shock rather than a gradual drift, amplifying market impact when it hits.
insights.quote: when the GPIF does something, it gives a green light to everybody else who was thinking about doing the same thing

insights.claim: Korea's late-2025 tax change exempting repatriated foreign gains and domestic gains from capital gains tax (while taxing future foreign holdings) drove Korean investors out of US growth names and helped send the Korean market 'bananas'.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A proven policy template that other Asian governments (notably Japan) could copy, redirecting flows from US growth to domestic assets.
insights.quote: if you sell now... and repatriate your money and buy domestic assets... you get capital gains free

insights.claim: The Godzilla (yen repatriation) risk is cheaply hedgeable today via out-of-the-money yen calls because FX volatility is unusually low in 2026 despite geopolitical uncertainty, and the yen tends to move ~10 big figures in a week every 7-10 years.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Actionable, low-cost portfolio insurance: buy yen calls now while FX vol is depressed to hedge repatriation and dollar-decline scenarios.
insights.quote: today the V in FX is is very cheap so you can you can hedge yourself against the Godzilla effect by buying calls on the yen

insights.claim: A yen call hedge works double-duty because in an AI bust the dollar likely tanks (the US runs ~12% of GDP twin deficits and needs the AI 'exciting story' to attract foreign capital, while a bust would force the Fed to slash rates).
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: One cheap hedge (yen calls) covers both of Gave's two main tail risks, an attractive risk/reward for the AI bust that is otherwise hard to hedge.
insights.quote: if the AI story goes bust I think the dollar goes down partly because if AI goes bust the Fed's going to crush interest rates

insights.claim: Shorting semiconductors or buying NASDAQ puts are poor AI-bust hedges (you 'die until you're right' and puts are expensive on high vol), making the cheap yen call the preferred way to hedge an AI unwind.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Direct AI-bust hedges carry high carry/timing risk; redirect hedging budget to the cheaper, correlated yen trade.
insights.quote: buy puts on the NASDAQ but the volatility is pretty high. They're expensive

themes.theme: China: world-class companies, oil price-setter, DeepSeek threat

insights.claim: China is the price-setter of oil: it buys all it can around $65 and stops buying near $100, effectively capping oil in a $65-100 band that most of the world can live with.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: If China caps oil at ~$100, the inflationary-bust energy-spike scenario stays unlikely, reinforcing the inflationary-boom base case.
insights.quote: China buys as much oil as it can at 65 and stops buying at 100 and that's enough to regulate the market

insights.claim: China is for the first time producing genuine world-class companies (transportation, electricity generation/storage/transmission, telecoms, factory automation, robotics), comparable to Japan in the 1960s, shifting Gave from recommending Chinese bonds to Chinese equities.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: A structural bull case for select Chinese equities; the investable thesis has moved up the value chain from cheap commodity producers to quality global champions.
insights.quote: what you now have is the emergence of genuine world-class companies and this is new this is like Japan in the 1960s

insights.claim: China's economy is bifurcated: exports are booming on the cheapest cost of capital, electricity and labor plus an undervalued currency, while consumption stays depressed because consumer and business confidence were crushed by the real estate bust and government interference.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Play China's export/industrial champions, not its consumer; recovery needs the real estate balance-sheet drag to end.
insights.quote: China has the cheapest cost of capital, the cheapest cost of electricity and the cheapest cost of labor

insights.claim: China's stock market can rise despite a weak economy, just as US equities did from 2009-2018, because weak growth and low inflation give policymakers room for QE-style money printing that flows into equities.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Separate the China economy debate from the China market call; liquidity can drive Chinese equities higher even with poor GDP.
insights.quote: you have because you have fairly weak growth and because you have low inflation, there are no constraints to Chinese policy makers

insights.claim: Chinese government bonds are up about 5% in 2026 while US treasuries and German bunds are down, validating Gave's long-standing prior preference for Chinese sovereign debt.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Chinese sovereign bonds have diversified away from western bond weakness, a concrete relative-value win.
insights.quote: Chinese government bonds uh which by the way are up about 5% this year when US treasuries are down

insights.claim: DeepSeek's latest models are competitive with cutting-edge US LLMs, embodying China's 'Toyota/BYD' strategy of delivering ~95% of US model performance at ~5% of the cost, versus the US 'Ferrari' arms race to AGI and market dominance.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: If cheap Chinese models prove 'good enough', US hyperscaler capex could cool 20-30%, directly threatening the AI-capex boom the market depends on.
insights.quote: we're going to produce models that can do 95% of what the US models can do uh and can do it for 5% of the cost

insights.claim: US hyperscalers are effectively selling 'Ferraris for $2,000' by pricing AI products like Claude and ChatGPT far below cost to capture and lock in users, betting that hardware/compute scale wins and full pricing comes later.
insights.speaker: Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Current AI pricing is subsidized land-grab; the monetization assumption (users pay full price later) is the linchpin risk for the whole capex thesis.
insights.quote: all of us right now are getting, you know, anthropic or uh or CLA, you know, for for nuts for a lot less money than it should be given how much it costs to produce
